mnemonics: SAL: Set Alarm, Alert Later. Use alarm() to set a timer that will send a SIGALRM signal to your process later.
PAUSE for Signal: The pause() function makes your process sleep until it catches a signal, acting like a 'wait for interrupt' command.
keyPoints: alarm(unsigned int seconds) schedules a SIGALRM signal to be sent to the process after the specified number of seconds.
pause() suspends the calling process until any signal is delivered, causing a signal handler to be invoked or the process to terminate.
Only one alarm can be scheduled at a time; a new call to alarm() cancels any previously set alarm and returns the number of seconds remaining.
commonMistakes: Assuming alarm() will pause your program; it only schedules a signal, execution continues immediately.
Using pause() without setting a signal handler for SIGALRM first, which can lead to the process being indefinitely blocked if no other signals are received.
Forgetting that a new call to alarm() will overwrite any previously set timer, potentially disrupting the intended timing logic.
